GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "How Rey Vakili makes a perfect pair of dinner and drinks, care of Penfolds", "If you haven't been fine-tuning a few signature dishes at home, during this period of isolation, then you haven't been doing it right. The rise in domestic dining has been turning home cooks into (almost) Michelin star chefs the world over. But no great meal is complete without the perfect accompaniment–a glass of fine wine. To ensure her drop of choice was in harmony with the dishes she was making in her own kitchen, Rey Vakili took a masterclass in wine, courtesy of Sommelier and Penfolds Australian Ambassador, Zoe Warrington. Penfolds have been bottling their extensive list of wines in South Australia for more than 175 years. Zoe unveiled Penfolds beautiful Max's collection–a range designed to be enjoyed both right away or cellared for later–and schooled Rey in the perfect way to pair them. Penfolds Max's, first released in 2015, are named in honour of Penfolds first Chief Winemaker, Max Schubert. The pioneering vintner championed the notion of multi-region wines, arguing that sourcing the same grapes from different places, added a depth of flavour and sophistication to wine. That's the reason the bottles bearing the Max's label can be so complex, considering their price of $30. There are five wines featuring the elegant Max's label, including one white. "Max's Chardonnay has been one of my absolute favourites since its release. It is a hundred percent from the Adelaide Hills. It's got an almond, nutty complexity to it, so it pairs well with soft cheese, chicken and coconut curries and also with fish. There is a lot of scope with Chardonnay," explains Warrington. Max's Shiraz Cabernet is a classic Australian blend. "We were the first to do it, we're the benchmark," says Warrington. So you can trust the fact that Penfolds have this one down to a fine art. She described this one to Vakili as the "little black dress of wine – classic in style and something everyone should own.– It's an aromatic wine that straddles the line between savoury and sweet, so it goes well with a rich Ragu or grilled meats. Shiraz is a wine with big, bold tastes, says Warrington. It's rich, but unfussy. Max's Shiraz is made with fruit sourced from a few different regions in South Australia. "It's delicious and very accessible. It's on the sweeter spice spectrum, so things like nutmeg, cinnamon and ginger, as well as plum and chocolate," explains Warrington. The flavours perfectly complement a Sunday afternoon charcuterie plate, or Rey's truffle oil grilled cheese. For something with more oomph, Warrington suggests grabbing a bottle of her personal favourite of all the reds, Max's Cabernet Sauvignon. "This one is like a power pair of heels–confident, versatile, spicy and fun," she laughs. The notes of pink peppercorn, cranberry, plum, bay leaf and sage, pop with fatty meats (the tannins help break down the fat) or Chinese spices and duck. Of course, a wine-appreciation masterclass didn't just teach Rey how to make a perfect marriage out of a meal, and which wine goes with what. Warrington offered plenty of practical pearls of wine wisdom. Like the fact that reds should be enjoyed at 16-18 degrees, no warmer, if you want to get the most from the flavour. If your place is warmer than that, consider cooling it just a little. Chardonnay too, shouldn't go cooler than 10 to 12 degrees. If you insist on icy wine, she says to freeze grapes and drop those in your glass instead – they won't dilute the wine like melting ice can. Tune in to Vogue Australia's IGTV starting Thursday June 11th, for a mini masterclass on wine and the perfect pairing, with Rey Vakili, care of Penfolds. The remaining three episodes will air each Thursday thereafter."
